en.extract:
The savanna hawk (Buteogallus meridionalis) is a large raptor found in open savanna and swamp edges. It was formerly placed in the genus Heterospizias. It breeds from Panama and Trinidad south to  Bolivia, Uruguay and central Argentina. There are also reports of it in California, from 1973 in Alameda County and from 1974 in San Diego County.


Description

The savanna hawk is 46–61 cm (18–24 in) in length and weighs 845 g (29.8 oz). The adult has a rufous body with grey mottling above and fine black barring below. The flight feathers of the long broad wings are black, and the tail is banded black and white. The legs are yellow. The call is a loud scream keeeeru.
Immature birds are similar to the adults but have darker, duller upperparts, paler underparts with coarser barring, and a whitish supercilium. This species perches very vertically, and its legs are strikingly long.


Food and feeding
The savanna hawk feeds on small mammals, small birds, lizards, snakes, toads, frogs, eels, other fish, crabs, roots, spiders, and large insects (such as grasshoppers). It usually sits on an open high perch from which it swoops on its prey, but will also hunt on foot, and several birds may gather at grass fires.


Reproduction
The nest is of sticks lined with grass and built in a palm tree. The clutch is a single white egg, and the young take 6.5 to 7.5 weeks to fledging.


References


Additional sources
ffrench, Richard (1991). A Guide to the Birds of Trinidad and Tobago (2nd ed.). Comstock Publishing. ISBN 0-8014-9792-2.
F. Gary Stiles; Alexander Frank Skutch (1989). A guide to the birds of Costa Rica. Comstock Publishing. ISBN 0-8014-9600-4.


External links

Savanna hawk videos on the Internet Bird Collection
Savanna hawk photo gallery Archived 2011-06-14 at the Wayback Machine VIREO

The '''savanna hawk''' ('''''Buteogallus meridionalis''''') is a large [[Bird of prey|raptor]] found in open [[savanna]] and [[swamp]] edges. It was formerly placed in the genus ''Heterospizias''. It breeds from [[Panama]] and [[Trinidad]] south to  [[Bolivia]], [[Uruguay]] and central [[Argentina]].<ref name="sh-uwi-2014" /> There are also reports of it in California, from 1973 in Alameda County and from 1974 in San Diego County.<ref>{{cite web | url=https://ebird.org/checklist/S37650816 | title=EBird Checklist - 21 Sep 1974 - Tijuana River Valley--west of Hollister - 71 species (+4 other taxa) | date=21 September 1974 }}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:Buteogallus meridionalis -Goias, Brazil -flying-8.jpg|thumb|right|Flying in Goias, Brazil]]
The savanna hawk is {{convert|46|–|61|cm|abbr=on}} in length and weighs {{convert|845|g|abbr=on}}.<ref name="sh-book-hilty" /> The adult has a rufous body with grey mottling above and fine black barring below. The flight [[feather]]s of the long broad wings are black, and the tail is banded black and white.<ref name="sh-uwi-2014" /><ref name="sh-book-hilty" /> The legs are yellow. The call is a loud scream ''keeeeru''.<ref name="sh-book-hilty" />

Immature birds are similar to the adults but have darker, duller upperparts, paler underparts with coarser barring, and a whitish supercilium. This species perches very vertically, and its legs are strikingly long.<ref name="sh-book-hilty" />

==Food and feeding==
The savanna hawk feeds on small [[mammal]]s, small [[bird]]s, [[lizard]]s, [[snake]]s, [[toad]]s, [[frog]]s, [[eel]]s, other [[fish]], [[crab]]s, [[root]]s, [[spider]]s, and large [[insect]]s (such as [[grasshopper]]s).<ref name="UWI">{{cite web|url=https://sta.uwi.edu/fst/lifesciences/sites/default/files/lifesciences/images/Buteogallus_meridionalis%20-%20Savanna%20Hawk.pdf|title=Buteogallus meridionalis (Savanna Hawk)|website=Sta.uwi.edu|access-date=26 March 2022}}</ref><ref>{{cite web | url=https://www.peregrinefund.org/explore-raptors-species/hawks/savanna-hawk | title=Savanna Hawk | the Peregrine Fund }}</ref><ref>Ferguson-Lees, J. & Christie, D.A. & Franklin, K. & Mead, D. & Burton, P.. (2001). Raptors of the world. Helm Identification Guides.</ref> It usually sits on an open high perch from which it swoops on its prey, but will also hunt on foot, and several birds may gather at grass fires.<ref name="sh-uwi-2014" />

==Reproduction==
The [[bird nest|nest]] is of sticks lined with grass and built in a [[palm tree]].<ref name="sh-uwi-2014" /> The clutch is a single white egg, and the young take 6.5 to 7.5 weeks to fledging.<ref name="sh-book-hilty" />
